highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.212 Released: /fork Now Creates Background Sessions
highlights.summary: The `/fork` command has been redesigned to copy your conversation into a new background session visible in `claude agents`, letting you continue your current work without interruption. The old in-session behavior of `/fork` is now available under the new `/subtask` command. A new `claude auto-mode reset` command also lets you restore default auto-mode settings with a confirmation step.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.210: Live Timer for Long Tool Calls & Permission Rule Warnings
highlights.summary: Long-running tool calls now show a live elapsed-time counter in the collapsed summary line, so you can tell Claude is still working rather than stuck. Claude Code also now warns you at startup if your permission rules use `Write(path)` or `Glob(path)`, nudging you to use the safer `Edit(path)` or `Read(path)` alternatives instead.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.208: Screen Reader Mode & Vim Insert Remaps
highlights.summary: Accessibility gets a boost with a new opt-in screen reader mode that renders plain text — enable it via `claude --ax-screen-reader`, the `CLAUDE_AX_SCREEN_READER=1` env var, or the `axScreenReader` setting. Vim users can now map two-key insert-mode sequences like `jj` to Escape using the new `vimInsertModeRemaps` setting.

highlights.title: Claude Code Sends 33k Tokens Before Reading Your Prompt vs. OpenCode's 7k
highlights.summary: A developer investigation found that Claude Code consumes roughly 33,000 tokens of overhead before even processing your prompt, compared to about 7,000 for OpenCode — a significant cost difference for heavy users on API billing. If you are watching your token usage climb faster than expected, this systemic overhead is likely a key reason. Understanding this can help you budget usage or evaluate alternative frontends for cost-sensitive workflows.

highlights.title: Show HN: OtoDock – Run Claude Code and Codex as a Team of Agents on Your Server
highlights.summary: OtoDock is an open-source tool that lets you deploy Claude Code and OpenAI Codex together as a coordinated team of agents running on your own server or homelab. This is useful for developers who want persistent, server-side AI coding agents rather than running everything locally in a terminal. It targets intermediate-to-advanced users comfortable with self-hosting and VM environments.

highlights.title: Claude Code - Full Tutorial for Beginners
highlights.summary: A comprehensive beginner-focused YouTube tutorial that walks you through getting started with Claude Code from scratch. If you are new to Claude Code and unsure where to begin, this is a solid structured starting point covering the core workflow. Pair it with the official docs to reinforce what you learn.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: A concise 30-minute YouTube tutorial aimed at developers who want to get productive with Claude Code quickly without a multi-hour commitment. It is well-suited for intermediate users who already know the basics and want to level up their workflow efficiency. Watch it alongside the v2.1.212 release notes to apply the latest features immediately.

tips_of_the_day: Use `/fork` to spin off a long-running experiment into a background agent session while you keep chatting in your main session — check on it anytime via `claude agents` without losing your current context.

If your Claude Code API bill is higher than expected, audit your system prompt and permission rules: the tool sends significant token overhead per session, so keeping your CLAUDE.md and settings lean can meaningfully reduce costs.

Replace any `Write(path)` or `Glob(path)` permission rules in your settings with `Edit(path)` or `Read(path)` — Claude Code now warns you about these at startup, and the safer alternatives give you finer-grained control over what the agent can touch.
